terms.definition: Existence result: a feedforward network with at least one hidden layer, non-linear activation, and sufficient width can approximate continuous functions on compact input domains to any desired accuracy — canonical definition lives in this topic.

A model class (e.g. single-hidden-layer MLPs with sigmoid or ReLU) that can represent arbitrary continuous functions on bounded regions given enough units — distinct from guarantees about learning or generalization.
A closed and bounded subset of input space (e.g. [0,1]^d) on which classic UAT statements are stated; avoids unbounded inputs where approximation may fail without extra assumptions.
The number of neurons in the hidden layer whose count may grow as large as needed in the theorem; classic UAT uses one wide hidden layer rather than many narrow ones.

UAT proves an approximating network exists with some weights; it does not provide an algorithm to find those weights or bound how many samples are required — a common source of learner confusion addressed in this topic.
